aliases: change in the state of delivery
Change in the State of Delivery
市场结构转变（在伦敦反转背景下）
definition: A directional bias shift confirmed when price closes beyond the opening price/body of a consecutive series of same-direction candles (above down-close for bullish, below up-close for bearish). Signals the end of the prior delivery direction and the start of a reversal or continuation. The series becomes the order block for the new bias.
identification_rules: Identify a consecutive series of same-direction candles (all down-close or all up-close) on the working time frame (typically 5M, 15M, 30M, 1H, 4H, daily).

For a bullish CISD: wait for a candle to close above the opening price/high of the down-close series (use the first candle's opening if multiple). For a bearish CISD: wait for a candle to close below the opening price/low of the up-close series.
Confirmation requires a full candle body close beyond the series boundary — a wick touch, intrabar breach, or close that does not clear the series does not qualify.
If price fails to close beyond the series, the state of delivery has NOT changed; anticipate continuation in the original direction.
The entire series of same-direction candles that price closes beyond is marked as the candidate order block zone for the new directional bias.
The confirmed close should ideally occur with aggressive displacement (a strong, impulsive move), not a slow grind through the level.

The CISD is most valid when it occurs after a liquidity sweep (manipulation phase) in the opposing direction and aligns with a Kill Zone (London 02:00–05:00 EST or New York AM 08:30–12:00 EST) and a higher time frame PD Array or key level.
If the candle series is messy on the current time frame, step up one time frame (e.g., from 15M to 30M) to find a cleaner series for identification.
After a failed CISD attempt, re-identify the new series of same-direction candles formed after the failure — this new series becomes the updated order block for the eventual valid signal.

Apply fractally: use a lower time frame (e.g., 5-minute for hourly, hourly for daily) to confirm the higher time frame swing as protected; the closure on the lower time frame validates the higher time frame wick.
When SMT divergence is present on a correlated asset, a prior liquidity sweep is not strictly required.
If a new swing high/low forms before the closure occurs, abandon the old series and re-identify using the newest swing point.
Ensure the CISD aligns with the higher time frame bias; avoid countertrend CISD without clear higher time frame support.
In a reversal sequence, CISD typically follows Turtle Soup (liquidity sweep) and Inversion Fair Value Gap as the third confirmation step.
trading_implication: A confirmed CISD marks the end of manipulation and start of distribution in the Power of Three model. The series of candles closed beyond forms the order block for entry on retest. Enter at that order block or a fair value gap created by the displacement candle, place a stop beyond the manipulation swing extreme, and target external liquidity (Previous Day High/Low, session highs/lows, failure swings, equal highs/lows). CISD enables fractal alignment: a lower timeframe CISD validates the higher timeframe swing, allowing continuation trades targeting opposite liquidity or standard deviation projections for a minimum 2R. Without CISD, a swing remains unconfirmed and trades should not be taken.
common_mistakes: Calling a CISD on a wick breach rather than waiting for a candle close beyond the series of same-direction candles.
Identifying the CISD on the wrong time frame — using a higher time frame series when a lower time frame series is the operative one, or failing to step up when the current time frame series is messy.
Entering immediately on the CISD candle close without waiting for a retracement into the order block or fair value gap, reducing risk/reward quality.
Failing to first confirm a liquidity sweep (manipulation) in the opposing direction before looking for the CISD, leading to premature bias shifts.

Treating a CISD that occurs away from a Kill Zone or without a higher time frame PD Array as a valid trade trigger; a 'fake' CISD can occur to run liquidity in the original direction before the true reversal.
Confusing a London-session CISD with a New York Reversal setup when London has not yet reached a relevant higher time frame level.
Attempting to trade the CISD during London session rather than waiting for the New York session continuation, which offers a cleaner entry via the order block retest.
Treating a single candle close as sufficient without confirming a clear series of same-direction candles preceding it.
Ignoring the New York manipulation move (opposing run at 08:30 or 09:30) that often precedes the true continuation, causing premature entries in the wrong direction.
Confusing CISD with Market Structure Shift: CISD focuses on close through candle series opening, MSS on break of swing high/low.
Using the wrong candle as the reference when a series exists (should close through the first candle's opening, not the last).
Assuming a swing point is protected before a CISD confirms it.
Not updating the protected swing as new opposing candle series form after the initial CISD.
Ignoring the higher time frame bias, leading to trading against the dominant trend.
Taking a CISD signal that lacks aggressive displacement, often seen in slow, ranging consolidations.
Failing to reset the opposing candle series when a new swing high/low forms before closure.
Entering directly on the CISD without waiting for a retracement to an order block or fair value gap when risk-to-reward is poor.
Calling a CISD on a single candle without confirming a series (unless explicitly a single-candle series).
Using the last candle's opening instead of the first when multiple same-direction candles exist.
Ignoring the time of the CISD formation: a CISD formed late in the candle does not support expansion within that candle; wait for the next candle.
Simultaneously marking multiple PD Arrays (CISD, FVG, Breaker Block) leading to chart clutter and unclear entry points.
Expecting price to reach -2 or -2.5 standard deviation targets on large manipulation legs without considering the excessive range.
Confusing CISD with Candle 3 Closure; they are different patterns and should not be used interchangeably.
Using CISD without a preceding reversal sequence (Turtle Soup or Inversion FVG) when applicable.
Missing the use of old trend FVGs as support/resistance for new trend, missing reaccumulation or redistribution entries.
Judging CISD before high resistance liquidity is formed, leading to premature entries before low resistance liquidity is swept.
merge_notes: All four projects are ICT school; no school conflict. One project (Daily Profiles) uses 'CHoCH' as primary alias, while others use 'CISD'/'CSD'; unified card uses 'CISD' as primary alias. Definitions agree on close through opposing candle series; minor nuance: some specify first candle's opening, others general opening/body. Identification rules combined; special cases (SMT, fractal application, reset on new swing) preserved. Trading implications merged with emphasis on fractal alignment and standard deviation projections.
